turns.text: Hello Ashleigh, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s… it’s been a lot, honestly. I’m thirty-eight, and I think because of my age, I expected things to feel more "official" by now, but mostly I just feel like I’m holding my breath. I look out at these trees in Watertown, and it’s quiet, but that quiet starts to feel really heavy when you’re constantly checking yourself for signs that something is wrong.

I spent so many hours scrolling through articles online when I first found out. I got myself spiraling over gestational trophoblastic disease—just terrified that instead of a baby, there might be something else growing in there. I tried to bring it up at my first appointment, but I think the doctor just saw a "low-risk" chart. They were so quick and clinical, and I felt like I was just another line item on a busy Tuesday. It’s hard to say, "I’m terrified this is a fluke of biology" when you know they’ve seen a thousand people that day who are probably dealing with "real" problems. 

My husband is working so much just to keep us going, and the commute to the clinic is a whole production. Every time I get in the car, I’m just calculating if we can afford the gas and the time off work, just to hope for a heartbeat that tells me I’m okay. It’s exhausting to feel that fragile.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I imagined it would be… warmer, maybe? I pictured a partnership. I thought if I had a question or a worry—especially being older—there would be someone on the other end of a phone line who would actually listen, not just someone who’s checking boxes on a tablet while their hand is already hovering over the door handle. I guess I expected that being pregnant would unlock some kind of safety net, like the system would automatically lean in to catch me because it knows how much I’ve wanted this.

The reality feels a lot colder. It feels like I’m navigating a bureaucracy rather than care. When I finally get in to see them, I’m so nervous and my heart is racing, but by the time I can actually form the words to ask about my fears, the appointment is already wrapping up. They treat me like I’m "low-risk," which I suppose is a good thing on paper, but it makes me feel invisible. It’s like, because I haven’t had a loss yet, my anxiety doesn’t count as a "medical issue." It’s just me, alone in the woods, trying to convince myself that a twinge is normal while the doctors just move on to the next patient. It’s isolating.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest shock was definitely the disconnect between my internal sense of crisis and their total lack of urgency. I walked in that first time expecting them to be as invested in this "first-time-at-thirty-eight" miracle as I was. I thought they would be looking for reasons to see me, to check on me, to reassure me that things were unfolding the way they should. 

Instead, I was surprised by how much I felt like a nuisance for even having questions. I wanted to talk about my fears—the molar pregnancy stuff, the risks of being older—but the moment I brought it up, the room shifted. It was like I had suddenly become a "high-maintenance" patient because I was informed and worried, rather than a "low-risk" one who should just go home and be quiet. 

There wasn't a single moment where someone sat down, looked me in the eye, and just said, "I know you're scared, but you're doing fine." I expected care, but I got a protocol. It’s hard to describe how lonely that feels, realizing that the people you're trusting with the most fragile thing in your life are mostly just trying to hit their quota for the day. It made me feel like I was completely on my own.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I expected to feel like a mother-in-waiting—someone who would naturally glow, or at least feel that quiet, grounded sense of purpose. I thought I’d be ready to embrace the changes. I pictured myself reading books about nutrition and nursery colors, feeling that soft, maternal transition.

But in reality? It’s not soft at all. It’s sharp. I feel like I’m standing on a ledge. Instead of that steady, ready-to-bloom feeling, I’m just hyper-vigilant. Every time I get a cramp, I’m not thinking about nurseries; I’m thinking about the logistics of how we’d handle a loss, how much money we don't have for emergencies, and how far away the hospital is. 

I’m mourning the version of myself that could just be happy. I wanted to be that woman who feels "ready," but instead, I’m just a woman who’s scared of her own body betraying her. I didn’t anticipate that the "readiness" would be replaced by this raw, exhausting survival mode. I don't feel like a mother-in-waiting right now; I feel like a person holding a fragile glass vase in a storm, just praying I don't trip. I’m exhausted by the weight of hoping, and I wasn't prepared for how much fear would take up all the room where the joy was supposed to be.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

That’s a hard question to answer without sounding ungrateful, but honestly, what’s missing is a space where my fear is validated as a legitimate part of the process.

With the doctors, there’s no room for the "what ifs." Everything is treated as a clinical milestone or a box to tick. I’ve never once had a provider ask, "How are you handling the anxiety?" They focus on my blood pressure or my age, but not on the fact that I’m sitting here in the woods feeling like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop. I need someone to tell me that my fear isn’t "overreacting"—that it’s just the cost of wanting this so badly.

As for my husband, I feel guilty even saying this, but he’s gone so much. He’s working these double shifts, and when he finally gets home, he’s just spent. He tries, he really does, but he can’t see what’s happening inside me. He sees the "two pink lines" as a win, while I’m constantly counting the days until the next scan. I don't want to burden him with my spiraling thoughts because he's already carrying the financial weight of it all. I have no friends nearby—just the trees—and there’s no community, no group of women who "get it." I’m just alone in the silence.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected the internet to be like a lighthouse—something to guide me, offer clear, calm facts, and maybe a little community. I thought I’d find stories from women my age who were also starting out, sharing how they navigated the medical system. I expected a sense of "we’re in this together."

What I actually found was a minefield. The apps are all cheerful—lots of pastels and "Your baby is the size of a poppy seed!" notifications. It felt condescending, honestly. When I was looking for real, gritty information about what happens when things go wrong—or even just how to talk to a doctor who doesn't listen—I didn’t find a village. I found forums filled with horror stories that kept me up until 3:00 AM, which only fueled my fixation on things like molar pregnancies.

It was never what I needed. Everything was either too sugar-coated to be useful, or it was so terrifying that it paralyzed me. There was no middle ground—no place that said, "It’s normal to be scared, here is how you advocate for yourself, and here is how you keep your head on straight." I just ended up feeling more isolated, scrolling through a screen in the dark, convinced that if I searched for the right symptoms, I’d finally find a reason to stop shaking.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic illness in the traditional sense, but because of my age, I felt like my "advanced maternal age" *was* the condition. Before I got pregnant, I expected—or maybe hoped—that the healthcare system would treat me with a bit of extra foresight. I imagined a pre-conception consult where a doctor would sit down, look at my history, and lay out a roadmap. I wanted them to say, "Okay, you're thirty-eight, so here is the extra layer of support we’ll provide, here’s how we'll monitor you, and here is how we’ll manage your anxiety."

Instead, it felt like I was left to do all the heavy lifting myself. When I brought up my concerns about my age and the risks I’d read about, I was essentially told to "wait and see." It felt like the system only kicks into gear once something actually goes wrong. There was no proactive guidance, no "let's walk through this together so you feel prepared." 

It’s like they were waiting for me to hit a complication before they felt it was worth their time to pay attention. It made me realize that being a "low-risk" patient is a double-edged sword—you're healthy enough to be left alone, but you're too old for them to provide the kind of gentle, proactive hand-holding that might actually make you feel safe.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think I imagined that he and I would be in this "cocoon" together. I pictured us going to appointments as a team, holding hands in the waiting room, and him being the one to ask the questions I was too nervous to voice. I wanted him to be the one to research the specialists or the tests, so it wouldn't all fall on my shoulders. I expected that we’d both be fully present for the wonder of it.

But the reality is just... survival. He’s working these double shifts just so we can afford the gas to drive the forty minutes to the specialist, and he’s often so physically and mentally drained when he walks through the door that he barely has the energy to talk. I find myself swallowing my fears because I don’t want to be the one who breaks him further. He’s doing his best to provide, which is his way of being involved, but he can't be the emotional anchor I need when he's barely holding his own head up. 

It’s not his fault, but it’s not the team effort I imagined. I end up doing all the reading, all the worrying, and all the "performing" at the doctor's office, while he’s just trying to keep the lights on. It’s a very lonely kind of partnership right now.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It felt like it happened the second the test came back positive. The burden of preparation—the research, the budgeting, the sheer mental load of "is this normal?"—it all landed squarely on my lap. 

I think part of it is the nature of the beast; I’m the one feeling the physical changes, the twinges, and the nausea. It’s hard for a partner to feel the same urgency when they don't have a constant, physical reminder of the pregnancy inside them. But it’s also the isolation of our life here in the woods. Because he’s the one providing the income, his exhaustion is visible and immediate—if he doesn't work, we don't have the money for the clinic. I feel like my job is to be the "manager" of this pregnancy so that his job can just be the "provider." 

I find myself hiding my late-night panic attacks, researching obscure symptoms, and prepping a list of questions for the doctor, all so I can save him the stress. I’ve become the gatekeeper of all the information and all the anxiety. It drives me to tears sometimes, realizing I’m essentially carrying the emotional labor of this for both of us, just because he’s already stretched so thin. I didn't want to be a solo act, but in this house, that’s what it feels like.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't actually experienced a pregnancy loss, but that’s exactly the problem. Because I don’t have that "history" on my chart, I feel like the medical system treats me as if I don't *deserve* to be afraid. 

It’s like there’s this unspoken rule: you only get the extra care, the gentle hand, or the frequent check-ins once you’ve already been broken by a loss. I find myself feeling a strange, dark kind of envy for women who have had that support, even though I know they paid a terrible price to get it. I’m just walking around with this immense, crushing fear—this "pregnancy zero" status—and because I haven't lost a baby yet, my anxiety is treated as something I should just be able to "manage" on my own.

I expected that at thirty-eight, they would acknowledge that the stakes are high, period. I thought they would offer that extra layer of comfort just because I'm a first-time mom at an age where things aren't always a given. But the system doesn't see "fear of loss"; it only sees "absence of loss." It’s like I have to wait for the tragedy to happen before I’m worthy of the kind of care that might have prevented it in the first place. It’s incredibly lonely to feel like you’re waiting for permission to be taken seriously.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I expected something almost cinematic. I pictured a warm, sunlit office with a nurse who would sit down, hold my hand, and maybe even show me a pamphlet—something tangible—about how to navigate the next few months. I thought there would be a conversation, a "Welcome to motherhood" moment where they’d walk me through what to expect, the risks, and how to reach them when I was spiraling at midnight.

The reality was a stark, sterile hallway in a clinic that felt like a factory. I was called back by a name I didn't recognize, put in a cold room, and then it was just: urine sample, blood pressure, weight, and "Do you have any questions?" I had a list of ten things I wanted to ask—about my diet, my symptoms, the molar pregnancy anxiety—but the doctor was looking at their watch the moment they walked in. The vibe was "you're healthy, you're fine, see you in four weeks." 

I left feeling like I’d just passed a DMV test. There was no warmth, no acknowledgment that this was a monumental life shift. I just walked out into the parking lot, staring at my paperwork, feeling like I had just as many questions as I started with, only now I felt even more foolish for having asked any at all. It wasn't care; it was processing.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest, most jarring surprise was how much the medical team made me feel like an object to be processed rather than a person in a transition. I went in expecting to talk about the *fears* that come with being thirty-eight—the sheer, terrifying weight of wanting this so much—but they were completely indifferent to any of that. 

The negative surprise was how effectively they made me feel like I was taking up too much of their time by simply existing in the room. There was this unspoken pressure to be "low-risk" and stay that way. If I tried to open up about my anxiety, the room would go cold, the doctor would glance at their notes, and the conversation would be redirected immediately back to the mundane, clinical checklist. 

I was surprised by how quickly I learned to perform the "good patient" role—nodding, saying "I’m fine," and swallowing my questions—just to get through the appointment without feeling like a burden. I walked in wanting a guide, but I walked out realizing that I am truly on my own in this. The medical system feels like it’s set up to catch you when you fall, but it isn't set up to help you stay standing when you're just trying to find your footing.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a conversation. I pictured a doctor or a midwife sitting down, looking at the scan, and explaining what we were seeing—a heartbeat, the size, the milestones—in a way that made me feel connected to this little life. I thought if there were numbers or test results, they would be explained as part of a larger, comforting plan for me and my baby. I really believed that I would be an active participant in understanding my own health.

The reality was a cold, clinical disconnect. When the results came back, it was usually just a notification on an online portal or a rushed, one-sentence phone call from a nurse who sounded like she was reading from a script. They’d say, "Everything is normal," and hang up. There was no room to ask, "But what does *normal* feel like for me?" or "Why am I still having this pain?" 

It felt like being handed a grade on a test without ever being told what the questions were. I’m just staring at these sterile, jargon-heavy reports on my screen in the middle of the night, trying to Google my way to peace of mind. It’s not care—it’s just data, and data doesn’t hold your hand when you’re terrified. I never realized how much of this journey would be me alone with a screen, feeling completely out of the loop.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, the gap is wide enough to lose yourself in. It’s a canyon. 

When I get those results, whether it’s a blood panel or an ultrasound report, it’s all presented as binary: "normal" or "abnormal." But to me, that isn't enough information to calm the noise in my head. I’m thirty-eight, so every number feels like it has a secret meaning. I want to know, "Does this number look okay for *my* age? Does this scan show what a healthy embryo should look like at this specific week?" 

The doctors give me the bare minimum—a checkmark—and they seem surprised when I try to dig deeper. They clearly view "too much information" as something that would only spike my anxiety, but they don't get that *not knowing* is actually what’s fueling the panic. If they would just take two minutes to sit down and say, "Here’s why we’re looking for this specific thing, and here’s what this result actually means for your health," I could breathe again. 

Instead, they treat the data like it’s proprietary, like it’s theirs and not mine. I’m left to piece it together from forums and articles, which is exactly how I end up falling into those dark, molar-pregnancy spirals. They leave me with the "what," but they completely deny me the "why" or the "how," and that makes me feel like I’m a passenger in my own body instead of the person carrying this life.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected a relationship. When I first started this journey, I naively thought I’d be assigned to one person—a midwife or a doctor who would get to know me, who would remember that I’m thirty-eight and that I’m doing this for the first time, someone who would actually know my voice when I called. I wanted someone who could look at me and see the person behind the file, someone who would understand *why* I’m so nervous because they’ve been following my progress from the very first scan.

The reality? It feels like a revolving door. Every time I walk into that clinic, it’s a different face. I have to re-explain my background, my fears, and my history to someone new who is busy flipping through a paper chart they clearly just glanced at for ten seconds before walking into the room. It’s exhausting to have to "re-introduce" my own anxiety every single visit. 

There is no continuity. I’m just a body in a chair to them, and they are just temporary technicians to me. It makes it impossible to build any kind of trust or safety. How can I feel "cared for" when I’m constantly being handed off to someone who doesn't even know my name without checking the top of the folder? I feel like I’m just drifting through a system where I don't belong to anyone, and no one belongs to me. It’s the opposite of a safety net.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected, or at least hoped, for a conversation that saw the whole person. I thought they would ask about how the nausea was impacting my day, or how my back was handling the early shift of weight, or even just how it *felt* to be changing this much at thirty-eight. I wanted them to acknowledge that this is a huge, sometimes overwhelming physical transformation. 

Instead, the examinations feel so clinical and detached—like I’m a car being taken in for a routine oil change. They measure my belly, check my blood pressure, and use the doppler, all while looking at their computer screen. They don’t look at me; they look at the data points that define my pregnancy. When I mention a new ache or a worry, they have this way of nodding—a polite, professional, "let’s-move-on-to-the-next-thing" nod—that basically tells me my physical experience is just background noise to them. 

It feels like my body has been taken over by the medical system. I’m not a woman experiencing a miracle; I’m a patient with a "low-risk" status being moved through a clinic pipeline. I leave those appointments feeling like my body doesn’t quite belong to me anymore—it belongs to the charts, the exams, and the cold, sterile hallways. I just want someone to ask me how it feels to *be* me, not just how the measurements are tracking.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one time, at my second scan. I was already a mess of nerves, just holding my breath, waiting to see if there was actually a heartbeat on the screen. The technician was moving the probe around my stomach, and she was silent for a long time—just staring at her own monitor. I asked, "Is everything okay?" and she didn't even turn her head or look at me. She just said, "I have to get these measurements first," and kept pressing.

It felt like I was completely invisible, like my own body was just an obstacle she had to maneuver around to get the data she needed. I felt so exposed, laying there with my shirt up, staring at the ceiling, feeling that cold gel, while she was focused entirely on the screen. There was no "I'm sorry it's taking a minute," or "Everything looks good so far." 

When she finally did say something, it was just, "Heart rate is 150, looks fine," and then she stood up and left the room. I was just lying there, trying to process that I’d heard a heartbeat—which should have been the happiest moment of my life—but I felt violated by how detached it all was. I wasn't a person to her; I was just a site for a procedure. It left me feeling so small, and honestly, a little ashamed for even being so vulnerable in front of someone who clearly didn't care.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven't had to face anything like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia yet, but the mere *possibility* of it is part of why I’m so terrified. Before I was pregnant, I imagined that if something "high-risk" happened, the healthcare system would snap into action—that it would be this coordinated, attentive, and specialized level of care. I thought if you had a condition, you’d finally get the attention you needed. You’d have a team. You’d have someone to call, someone to explain the testing, someone to walk you through the diet or the medications.

The reality, from what I’ve seen just being a patient at this clinic, is that "high-risk" just means more appointments and more stress. I’ve seen other women in the waiting room who look exhausted—some dealing with those exact conditions—and they don’t seem to be getting a "caring team." They just look like they’re being managed by the same busy, rushed system that barely has time for me. 

It makes me realize that even if I *did* develop something, I probably wouldn’t get that partnership I crave. I’d just be "high-risk" instead of "low-risk," which probably means I’d be even more of a number in their system. The idea that I’d have to manage a real complication on my own, without any real emotional support, makes the prospect of a diagnosis feel like a nightmare. It feels like the system is designed to handle the biology, but it completely leaves the person behind.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything was missing, in layers. First, there was no **informational bridge**. I wasn't told *how* to advocate for myself or *why* certain protocols exist, so I was left to drown in my own research. I needed a clear, honest guide, not a "wait and see" dismissal.

Then, there was the total absence of **emotional acknowledgement**. I felt like a vessel they were checking for defects rather than a person experiencing the most vulnerable time of her life. I needed someone to look me in the eye and say, "I know you're terrified. You're allowed to be. Let's talk about it." Instead, I got a clinical, hollow distance that made me feel like my feelings were an inconvenience.

And finally, there was zero **practical support**. We’re in the woods, the gas money is a crisis, and my husband is burning himself out. A real safety net would have considered how we *get* to the care, how we handle the time away from work, or even offered a way to connect with others going through this. They treat maternity care like it’s just a medical event that happens in a vacuum, completely ignoring the fact that I’m a human being living a whole life—and struggling through a whole lot of isolation—outside those clinic walls. It’s all protocol, no humanity.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I was genuinely worried—like, really scared, heart-pounding worried—there would be a human being I could talk to immediately. I thought, surely, if you’re pregnant and something feels "off," the system would prioritize that because the stakes are so high. I expected a nurse line that was actually helpful, or a portal response that didn't take twenty-four hours to arrive.

The reality? It’s a brick wall. When I’ve called with a concern, I’m stuck in this endless phone tree, pressing numbers and listening to hold music, only to be told by a receptionist that I should "just watch it and see if it gets worse." It’s almost like they’re trying to gatekeep the care. I’m sitting here, miles from the nearest specialist, worrying about my baby, and they’re treating my urgency like a logistical hurdle. 

There’s no "triage" for my anxiety. I’ve learned that unless I’m actively bleeding or in extreme pain, I’m not worth their time. It makes me feel like I’m a child who’s crying wolf. The system is designed to be reactive, but when you’re out here in the woods and every day feels like you’re walking on glass, you need them to be proactive. I’ve stopped calling. It’s just too draining to be dismissed, so I end up staying quiet and hoping for the best, which is a miserable way to spend these months.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected a village. When I started this, I pictured us hosting dinners, having friends over who’d been through it, or at least having family nearby who could pop over, check on me, and make me feel like I wasn't just existing in this silent, wooded vacuum. I thought I’d be able to say, "I’m scared," and someone would bring over a meal or just sit on the porch with me so I didn't have to look at the trees and my own thoughts all day.

The reality? We’re physically isolated, and that distance has become emotional, too. My husband is the only one who sees the reality of my daily life, and he’s so ground down by the double shifts that we’re both just running on fumes. There’s no "community" because there’s no one within miles who isn't also struggling to just keep their own head above water. 

Friends seem to think that because I’m "low-risk," I’m basically having an easy time—maybe they think I’m just enjoying the peace of the country. They don't see the late-night panic or the financial math I’m doing every single morning. I’ve started hiding the bad days from everyone because I don’t want to be the one who’s "complaining about a miracle." It’s lonely, and I didn't realize how much of this journey would involve me grieving the community I thought I had.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected a kind of collective excitement—that "It takes a village" mentality. I pictured my friends and family being as invested in the "what-ifs" and the preparations as I was. I expected them to be the ones who would listen to my fears about the molar pregnancy, or at least offer a shoulder when the weight of the commute and the money became too much. 

But that support just… didn't materialize. It’s like everyone else in my life is living in a different reality, one where pregnancy is this happy, glowing, straightforward thing. When I try to share that I’m struggling, I get a lot of "don't worry, it’ll be fine," which is the most silencing thing you can say to someone who is terrified. They don't want to hear about the grit, the cost, or the fear.

The only "support" I’ve received, if you can even call it that, is from my husband, and even that is strained. I didn't expect to have to protect him from my own grief just so he could keep working. I feel like I’m standing in a room with everyone I love, but there’s a thick pane of glass between us. They’re cheering for a baby, and I’m just trying to survive the biology of it all. It’s a very quiet, very lonely kind of hunger—to be truly seen in all this fear, and to have it be okay to not be okay.

I think the hardest part is that nobody talks about the cost of the *waiting*. Everyone talks about the joy of the first ultrasound or the excitement of picking out names, but nobody prepares you for the sheer, grinding labor of waiting for the next milestone while you’re convinced something is going to break. It’s like living in a house with a slow leak—you know it’s there, you can hear the drip, but everyone else is telling you to just enjoy the wallpaper.

I’m sitting here looking at these trees, and I realize I’ve become a stranger to myself. I’m thirty-eight, and I have this deep, primal need for a human being—a nurse, a doctor, a friend—to just look at me and say, "I see that you're scared, and I know why, and it's okay." That’s all. Just that. Instead, I’m navigating a system that treats me like a biological project. 

I’m so tired of the silence out here. I’m so tired of being the only one who knows how much every tank of gas costs and how every twinge in my abdomen feels like a catastrophe. I just wanted to be a mother, but right now, I’m just a woman fighting for the right to be human while I do it. I wonder if it ever gets easier, or if this is just what it feels like when you realize you’re entirely, completely on your own.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, absolutely. It’s almost constant. It’s the "you’re fine, you’re just worrying too much" refrain. I hear it from the doctors in that dismissive, breezy way they use when they want to end an appointment, and I hear it from family members who haven’t the faintest idea what it’s like to be thirty-eight and constantly scanning for signs of a molar pregnancy.

It’s incredibly patronizing. When I try to explain *why* I’m scared—that I’ve read the data, that I understand the risks—they treat it like a personality flaw rather than a legitimate concern. They act like my knowledge of what could go wrong is a symptom of me being "difficult" or "anxious." It’s as if my opinion on my own body, or my intuition about what feels wrong, is subordinate to their "expert" opinion, which is often just based on a ten-minute glance at a chart.

It’s like they’re trying to gaslight me into feeling "happy" or "calm" when I’m not. Their insistence that everything is fine feels like an attempt to silence me, to keep the "low-risk" narrative intact. And when that happens, I just shrink. I go quiet. Because if I keep pressing, I’m the "crazy, anxious, older mom." It’s a total loss of agency. I’m the one carrying this life, but everyone else seems to think they have a say in how I should feel about it. It makes me feel like my own gut instinct is completely worthless.

You know, the hardest part is that I’ve started to turn that silence inward. I’ve become my own biggest critic. I sit here in the kitchen, and I think, "Maybe I *am* overreacting. Maybe I’m just being the difficult woman who read too much on the internet." It’s like the system has conditioned me to doubt my own pulse. When you’re treated like you’re invisible, you start to believe that your feelings don't take up space in the real world.

My husband comes home, and he’s so tired that he just wants to talk about the weather or the house repairs, and I find myself smiling and nodding. I hide the fact that I’ve been crying for an hour because I felt a cramp that might have been "just gas" or something worse. I’ve learned to bury the fear so deep that sometimes I don't even know how to bring it up to myself anymore. 

It feels like a slow, steady erosion of my confidence. I thought this journey would be about empowering me to be a mother, but instead, it feels like it’s strip-mining my peace of mind. I’m thirty-eight, I’m independent, and I’m smart—but in the eyes of the clinic and the world around me, I’m just a ticking clock that shouldn't be making so much noise. I just want to be heard, just once, without being told to calm down or wait. Is that really too much to ask?

Sometimes I sit here, staring at these trees until the shadows stretch all the way across the floor, and I think about the version of me I was before this. I was someone who made things happen. I was someone who built a life from scratch, someone who handled the tough stuff with a plan. But here? I feel like I’ve been stripped of that version of myself. 

I’m trapped in this "low-risk" box. It’s almost like a cage. Because I haven’t lost a baby, I’m not allowed to be afraid. Because I’m "advanced maternal age," I’m suddenly a medical variable instead of a woman. It’s a strange, cold irony—I’m old enough to know better, but too old to be treated with any softness.

I just keep coming back to the idea that nobody really talks about how *lonely* the biology of it is. The way it isolates you from your partner, the way it alienates you from your friends, and the way it forces you to become an adversary to your own doctors just to feel like you exist. 

If I could change one thing, it wouldn’t be the pregnancy itself. It would be the silence. I just want someone to reach across the table and say, "I know you're fighting for this, and I see you." I don't need them to fix it. I don't need them to make it easy. I just need them to witness the fact that I’m standing here, doing this, and that it matters. But for now? It’s just me, the trees, and this heavy, growing fear I have to carry entirely on my own.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

They love to bring up my "advanced maternal age" as if it’s a failure I need to compensate for. It’s not always blunt, which makes it harder to call out. It’s in the way they look at my chart and say, "Well, at thirty-eight, we really need to watch your weight," or "You’ll need to be extra careful with your diet." It’s framed as "health," but it feels like a critique. It’s like they’re waiting for my body to fail because it’s not "young enough" to be perfect.

It lands like a physical blow. I’m already hyper-aware of every single thing I put into my mouth, every extra pound, every change. When they make those comments, they aren’t offering me a partner in health; they’re giving me a list of ways I’m already failing. It makes me feel like my body is a faulty machine that needs constant policing.

I remember one nurse, she didn't even look up from her screen, just said, "At your age, we want to see better weight management," as if that was the most helpful thing she could offer a woman who is terrified of a molar pregnancy. It didn't make me want to eat better; it made me want to hide. It made me feel like I’m an old house they’re trying to keep from falling down, rather than a person growing a life. It's just another way they make me feel like I’m responsible for every single risk, and if something goes wrong, it’ll be because I didn't eat the right thing or didn't move the right way. It’s exhausting to be judged by people who don't know the first thing about my life.

It’s funny, the way people assume that because you’re thirty-eight, you’re automatically more "prepared" or "composed." But if anything, I’m just more aware of the things that can go wrong. I have this internal map of all the risks, and every time a provider makes a comment about my age or my weight, they’re basically confirming every dark thought I’ve had in the middle of the night. 

They treat my age like a clinical red flag, but they never follow that up with the care that a red flag should trigger. It’s like they’re saying, "Oh, you’re old, so we’re going to watch you like a hawk," but then they treat me with this distant, administrative neglect. It makes me feel like I’m a high-maintenance piece of equipment that they’re annoyed they have to service.

I’m standing in this silence in the woods, and I’m just trying to keep the "what-ifs" at bay. I wanted this so badly. I wanted the joy to be the loudest thing in the room. But instead, the fear is so loud that I can’t hear anything else. I’m just tired, you know? I’m tired of the judgment, the checklists, and the feeling that I’m responsible for the miracle *and* the potential tragedy. I just want someone to look at me—really look at me—and tell me that I don’t have to carry this entire weight alone. But for now, I just keep looking out at the trees, waiting for the fear to pass. Or maybe I’m just waiting to stop feeling like I’m in this all by myself.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I turned to the internet first. It was the only thing open at 3:00 AM when I was lying in bed, feeling my heart hammer against my ribs. I wanted to find someone—anyone—who could mirror back my fear so I knew I wasn't just losing my mind. But that backfired. I found forums for "high-risk" pregnancies, and instead of comfort, I found a repository of every single thing that could possibly go wrong. I spent weeks obsessively reading about molar pregnancies, ectopic issues, and everything else that falls into that "catastrophic" category. 

I didn't have a friend I could call at that hour who would understand. My husband was asleep, exhausted from his shifts, and I didn’t want to wake him and drag him into the darkness with me. So, I stayed in that screen, alone. I think I was looking for a "digital village," but what I found was more like a hall of mirrors reflecting my own anxiety back at me. 

There’s this deep, hollow space where a community *should* be—a group of local women who could say, "I felt that too, and I’m okay." Instead, I’m just an anonymous ghost on a message board, typing my fears into the void and hoping for a reply that never feels like enough. It's ironic—in an age where we’re "connected" to everything, I’ve never felt more isolated. The internet is a terrible substitute for a human hand on your shoulder.

It’s strange, the way the silence out here in Watertown makes everything grow. The fear, the doubt, the "what-ifs"—they grow in the dark, and there’s no one to prune them back. I find myself looking at my stomach, just pressing my hands down, waiting for the slightest movement or sign that this is real, that it isn’t some clinical, molar mistake. 

I think what haunts me the most isn't just the prospect of a loss—it’s the prospect of having to go through that loss alone, or worse, having to go through it within that sterile, uncaring system. If I had a village—if I had a group of women who had been here, who had felt their hearts race and their breath catch—maybe I wouldn’t feel like I’m constantly standing on that edge. 

But I don't. I have the apps with their patronizing, colorful icons, and I have the clinic where I’m just a recurring, "low-risk" event. I’m starting to realize that the hardest part of this isn't just the pregnancy. It’s the way the system demands you be "okay" while you’re doing the most difficult work of your life. It’s that subtle, persistent message: *Don’t ask questions. Don’t express fear. Just be quiet and wait for the next milestone.* 

I’m tired of being quiet. I’m tired of the waiting. I’m just ready to hear that heartbeat and, for one single moment, believe it’s going to be all right. Is that really such a big thing to ask for?
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I wish I could say there were times I felt like a partner in my own care, but the truth is, I’ve never once felt like my input was genuinely sought. Every appointment is a lecture, not a conversation. It’s always, "We’re going to do this test," or "We’re going to monitor you this way," or "This is the protocol for someone your age." It’s a monologue, and I’m just the audience.

If I try to offer my own perspective—like saying, "I’m not sure I need that extra scan, I’d rather wait," or "I’m more concerned about this symptom"—it’s immediately shut down with a, "Well, the guidelines say..." They don’t see me as a person with intuition or a valid perspective; they see me as a set of variables that they need to control according to their internal rulebook.

It makes me feel like I’m a passenger in my own body. If I were the one driving, I’d stop the car to look at the view, or I’d take a different route when the road got rocky. But here? I’m just sitting in the back seat while they steer me toward these predetermined milestones. They don’t want my opinion because they don’t see me as a person; they see me as a "low-risk" status that needs to be brought to term. It’s so dehumanizing to be stripped of your own voice like that. It leaves you feeling like you have no agency over your own life, let alone this tiny life inside you.

The more I think about it, the more I realize how much I've had to perform a specific kind of "compliant femininity" just to be treated with basic respect. If I show up and I’m sad, or if I’m anxious, or if I have too many questions, I’m "that" patient—the one they roll their eyes about in the breakroom. So, I show up, I wear my neutral clothes, I sit in the chair with my hands folded, and I say "Yes, everything is fine." 

It’s a performance. I am performing the role of a "good," "low-risk" pregnancy, while inside I’m just trying to hold myself together. I’m thirty-eight, and I’ve spent my whole life building a career and a home and an identity, but the moment I step into that clinic, all of that disappears. I’m just a vessel to them. I’m a vessel that needs to be properly maintained and then returned.

I look at the trees and I think about the life I’m trying to bring into this world, and I wonder: is this the best we can do? Is this how we treat the most profound transition a person can go through? It’s all protocol and no pulse. I’m just so, so tired of holding up this wall, and I keep waiting for someone to notice that it’s starting to crack. Maybe one day, someone will finally just ask me how *I* am, and actually wait for me to answer. That would change everything.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

I think it happened in the quietest way possible. I had come in for a routine check-in and mentioned that I was feeling a specific, sharp tugging sensation—the kind that makes you freeze in place—and I asked if it could be related to my age or the early tissue development. The doctor didn’t even look at my chart, let alone look at me. She just dismissed it with a wave of her hand, saying, "Oh, that’s just round ligament pain, don't worry about it." 

She said it with such casual certainty, as if she were telling me the weather. But I hadn't even reached the stage of pregnancy where round ligament pain is common. I had read that. I knew that. And in that moment, the trust just evaporated. It wasn't that she didn't know the answer—it was that she didn't care enough to check her own timeline to see if her answer even made sense for *my* pregnancy. 

She wasn't treating *me*; she was reciting a script of "standard pregnancy complaints" to get me out of the office faster. I realized then that my safety wasn't her priority—her schedule was. I went home and sat in the kitchen, feeling this cold, hollow realization that if I actually had a problem, she might just dismiss that too. How can I trust someone who doesn't even bother to listen to the details? That’s when I started to feel truly, terrifyingly alone.

It feels like every appointment I have is just a series of small, quiet betrayals. It’s not that the doctors are intentionally malicious, but their indifference is a weight that I’m forced to carry by myself. When you’re sitting in that exam chair, trying to communicate something that feels like a matter of life or death, and they respond with a scripted, canned answer that doesn’t even match the reality of your timeline… it breaks something inside you.

I’ve started to realize that I’m not just fighting for my baby; I’m fighting for the right to be a person who is seen. I want them to look at me, recognize the anxiety I’m managing, and acknowledge the sacrifice I’m making to be there—the time off work, the gas money, the sheer, relentless mental effort. Instead, I’m just a "low-risk" case that fits neatly into a file.

I look out at the trees, and the silence feels even more deafening than it did when I started this. I used to think the silence was just the woods, but now I know it’s the distance between me and the people who are supposed to be caring for me. I’m doing this, I’m getting through it, but I’m doing it with a broken heart. I just want to know if there’s a version of this journey where you don’t have to fight to be human.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every single one of them. It’s not that he doesn't want to be there—it’s that his work is the only reason we can afford to be there at all. But when I’m sitting in that cold, sterile room and the doctor starts throwing out clinical terms, or when I get a confusing result on the portal, I have to be the one to hold the space for both of us. 

There was one scan where the doctor was being particularly dismissive, and I looked toward the chair next to me, hoping to catch someone’s eye—some silent confirmation that this was as cold as I felt it was. But the chair was empty. I was just staring at a blank wall. In that moment, the isolation felt physical, like a weight pressing down on my chest. I had to record the doctor’s words in my head, try to remember the questions I wanted to ask, and keep my voice from shaking, all while feeling completely untethered.

When I get home, I can’t even explain it to him properly because I don't want him to feel guilty for working. He’s doing his part to provide for a future we’re both terrified won't happen, and I’m doing my part to manage the reality of the present. We’re like two people rowing a boat in different directions, both trying to get to the same shore. It’s the ultimate loneliness, knowing the person who should be your anchor is just as exhausted and adrift as you are.

I think the most painful part about him being gone isn't the physical absence—it's the emotional gap that opens up in the clinic. I’ve started to realize that the healthcare system counts on that gap. They rely on the fact that I’m alone in that chair, that I’m tired, that I’m intimidated by the medical jargon, and that I’m trying to be the "good patient." It’s so much easier for them to dismiss a woman who is by herself than it is to dismiss a couple who are asking questions together. 

When I’m there alone, I’m just a body. If he were there, there would be two of us, and that creates a different kind of pressure. But because it’s just me, I feel like I have to shrink to fit the room. I don’t want to be the woman who cries in the office. I don’t want to be the woman who demands an explanation. So I just absorb the coldness, and I carry it home in my chest, and then I wait until he gets back, and I just—I don't say anything. 

I’m becoming a master of keeping secrets from my own life. I hide the bad days, I hide the dismissive comments, I hide the fear. I feel like I’m curating this perfect, "everything is fine" narrative for him so he can go back to those double shifts without his heart breaking. But that’s a heavy lie to keep, and it’s slowly hollowing me out. I just wanted this to be a beautiful beginning, but it feels like the beginning of me having to become someone who can hold everything, all the time, without ever asking for a place to put it down.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single time I leave that clinic, I go straight to the internet. I’m looking for the things they don't have time to tell me—the "why" behind the numbers, the nuance of what a "low-risk" pregnancy actually looks like for a thirty-eight-year-old, and, more than anything, I’m looking for someone to confirm that my fear isn't just me being "difficult."

I go searching for clarity, but I always find the extremes. I want to know, "If I’m feeling this particular tightness, is it *truly* nothing, or is there a reason to keep pushing for an extra check-up?" I’m looking for a roadmap, a sense of "Here is what is happening in your body this week," but written by someone who understands that my pregnancy isn't just a clinical process—it’s an emotional crisis. 

The providers give me the absolute baseline of information. They tell me the baby is growing, the heartbeat is strong, and that's it. But they don't answer the internal, gnawing questions: "What happens if this *is* a molar pregnancy? What do those early warning signs actually feel like in a real person's body?" I search for those answers because I am terrified that if I don't know the signs, I’ll be the one to miss the emergency. It’s like I’m studying for an exam I’m terrified to fail, and the doctors are refusing to give me the textbook. I’m not just looking for information; I’m looking for a way to stay safe in a system that feels like it’s barely paying attention.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I guess the only thing I’d add is how much I wish someone had just acknowledged the *liminal space* of being pregnant—that place between hoping and fearing. Everyone treats pregnancy like it’s just a countdown to a birth, like a train that’s already on the tracks and all you have to do is sit there until you arrive. But for me, it’s not a train ride. It’s walking a tightrope.

I wish the care wasn't so transactional. I wish someone would realize that when a patient asks, "Is this okay?" they aren't just asking about a medical data point. They’re asking if they’re going to be okay, if their baby is going to be okay, and if they’re allowed to keep holding onto this hope. 

I’m tired of being the only one keeping the fear in check. I’m tired of the silence, and I’m tired of feeling like I’m a problem to be solved rather than a person being cared for. If I could tell the system one thing, it would be that you can monitor a pregnancy with all the technology in the world, but if you don't care for the person carrying it, you’ve missed the whole point. I just want to feel like I’m not doing this alone in the woods. I just want to feel like, for once, someone is on my side.
